course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
title: Exam 1 Review: Vectors, Matrices, Systems, Subspaces, Transformations, and Determinants
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard. This is not a student worksheet.
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, verbal, and board-friendly. Avoid textbook phrasing unless the problem needs it.
sessionGoal: Students should leave with a clean map of Exam 1 skills and practice the most common mixed problem types from the first part of the course.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Open with a topic map: vectors/matrices, systems/RREF, subspaces/bases, transformations, determinants.
Tell students the goal is not to memorize; it is to recognize the problem type quickly.
Do Problems 2, 3, and 5 if time is short.
For each problem, ask: what type of problem is this before solving?
End by pointing students to Practice Lab Sections 1-4 and Exam Mode.
sayThisFirst: Exam review is about recognition. First identify the problem type, then use the matching method.
For vector problems, ask: do I need a vector, a scalar, or a geometric interpretation?
For systems, row reduce and read pivots/free variables.
For subspaces and bases, ask: do I need span, independence, or both?
For transformations, remember columns are images of basis vectors.
boardPlan: Make a two-column board: Topic / What to do first.
List the five problem types in today’s review.
Work through the problems in order, but skip Problem 1 if students are already strong with vectors.
After each problem, write one takeaway sentence.
Leave the last 5 minutes for students to choose which Practice Lab sections to review.

examCoverageGuide.whatToRecognize: Component arithmetic, dot products, matrix dimensions, transposes.
Pivots, free variables, no solution vs infinitely many solutions.
Span membership equations and independence equations look similar but ask different things.
Build matrices from images of basis vectors and use Ax.
Use det(A) to decide invertibility and track row operation effects.

commonMistakes: Not saying what type of problem it is before starting algebra.
Using RREF columns as a basis for Col(A).
Mixing up span membership with linear independence.
Forgetting that T(e_i) values become columns.
For determinant parameter problems, giving the bad value instead of the invertible condition.
notationWarnings: Do not call det(A) a matrix. It is a scalar.

problems.title: Vector warm-up: arithmetic and dot product meaning
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \(u=(2,-1,2)\) and \(v=(1,3,0)\). Compute \(u+2v\), compute \(u\cdot v\), and decide whether the angle between \(u\) and \(v\) is acute, obtuse, or right.
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: first check whether each answer should be a vector or a scalar.

drawInstructions: Draw two arrows from the origin labeled u and v.
Write dot product < 0 means the angle is obtuse.
Do not try to draw exact 3D coordinates; use the sign interpretation.

walkthrough.label: Interpret the angle
walkthrough.latex: Because \(u\cdot v<0\), the angle between them is obtuse.

problems.finalAnswerLatex: u+2v=(4,5,2),\qquad u\cdot v=-1,\qquad \text{the angle is obtuse.}
commonMistakes: Thinking dot product gives a vector.
Forgetting to multiply v by 2 before adding.
Only saying dot product is negative without interpreting the angle.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 1 for vector/matrix basics.

problems.title: Read a solution set from RREF
problems.studentPromptLatex:
The augmented matrix of a system row-reduces to
\[\begin{bmatrix}1&0&2&|&-1\\0&1&-3&|&4\\0&0&0&|&0\end{bmatrix}.\]
Write the solution set in parametric form.
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: this problem is about reading, not more row reducing. Identify pivots and free variables first.

drawInstructions: Write x3=t above the free column.
Translate the first two rows into equations.
Box the final parametric vector form.

walkthrough.label: Identify pivot and free variables
walkthrough.latex: Pivots are in columns 1 and 2, so \(x_1\) and \(x_2\) are pivot variables. Column 3 has no pivot, so let \(x_3=t\).

commonMistakes: Forgetting to introduce a parameter for x3.
Thinking the zero row means no solution. It does not.
Leaving the answer as equations without a clear solution set.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 2 for RREF and solution sets.

problems.title: Span, dependence, and a basis for the span
problems.studentPromptLatex:
Let
\[v_1=(1,0,2),\quad v_2=(0,1,-1),\quad v_3=(2,1,3),\quad b=(3,2,4).\]
Is \(b\) in \(\operatorname{span}\{v_1,v_2,v_3\}\)? Are \(v_1,v_2,v_3\) linearly independent? Give a basis for their span.
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: this problem asks three related questions. Do not answer only the first one.

drawInstructions: Draw a plane through the origin spanned by v1 and v2.
Draw v3 inside the same plane and label v3=2v1+v2.
Draw b inside the same plane and label b=3v1+2v2.
Emphasize that v3 is redundant, so v1 and v2 form a basis for the span.

walkthrough.label: Check b is in the span
Decide independence
walkthrough.latex: Since \(v_3=2v_1+v_2\), one vector is made from the others. Therefore the set is linearly dependent.

walkthrough.label: Give a basis for the span
walkthrough.latex: The vectors \(v_1\) and \(v_2\) are not multiples of each other, so they are independent. Since \(v_3\) is redundant, a basis is\n\[\{v_1,v_2\}=\{(1,0,2),(0,1,-1)\}.\]

problems.finalAnswerLatex: b\in\operatorname{span}\{v_1,v_2,v_3\},\quad \{v_1,v_2,v_3\}\text{ is dependent},\quad \text{a basis for the span is }\{(1,0,2),(0,1,-1)\}.
commonMistakes: Answering span membership but forgetting independence.
Thinking dependent means b is not in the span. These are different questions.
Giving all three vectors as a basis even though v3 is redundant.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 3 for span, independence, and bases.

problems.title: Linear transformation and area scaling
skills: Use determinant for invertibility and area scaling
problems.studentPromptLatex:
Let \(T(x)=Ax\), where
\[A=\begin{bmatrix}1&2\\3&1\end{bmatrix}.\]
Compute \(T(2,-1)\). Is \(T\) one-to-one and onto? What is the area scale factor?
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: for a square 2 by 2 transformation, determinant answers invertibility and area scaling.

drawInstructions: Draw the unit square and its image as a parallelogram with area scale 5.
Write det(A)=-5, so orientation flips and area scales by 5.
Show T(2,-1) as a separate matrix-vector multiplication.

walkthrough.label: Interpret determinant
walkthrough.latex: Since \(\det(A)\neq 0\), \(T\) is one-to-one and onto. The area scale factor is \(|\det(A)|=5\). The negative sign means orientation is flipped.

problems.finalAnswerLatex: T(2,-1)=\begin{bmatrix}0\\5\end{bmatrix},\quad T\text{ is one-to-one and onto},\quad \text{area scale factor }=5.
commonMistakes: Forgetting absolute value for area scale.
Saying area scale is -5. Area cannot be negative.
Forgetting that determinant nonzero implies both one-to-one and onto for R2 to R2.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 4 for transformations and determinants.

problems.title: Parameter value for invertibility
problems.studentPromptLatex:
For what values of \(k\) is
\[M(k)=\begin{bmatrix}1&k&0\\2&1&1\\0&3&2\end{bmatrix}\]
invertible?
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: this is a determinant problem. Invertible means det is not zero.

drawInstructions: After computing -4k-1, set it equal to zero to find the bad value.
Draw a number line with k=-1/4 marked as the only excluded value.

walkthrough.label: Find the bad value
walkthrough.latex: The matrix is not invertible when\n\[-1-4k=0.\]\nSo \(k=-\frac14\).

commonMistakes: Stopping at k=-1/4 without saying whether that is good or bad.
Forgetting the minus sign in cofactor expansion.
Saying determinant must equal 1. It only needs to be nonzero.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 4 and Exam Mode for more review.

wrapUpQuestions: Which topics still feel slow: RREF, span/bases, transformations, or determinants?
What is the first thing you do when you see an RREF matrix?
What does determinant nonzero tell you?
What Practice Lab section should you review next?

linksToWebsite.reason: Linear systems, RREF, rank, and null space.

linksToWebsite.label: Practice Lab Section 3
linksToWebsite.reason: Subspaces, span, bases, column space, and null space.

linksToWebsite.label: Practice Lab Exam Mode
linksToWebsite.reason: Generate a mixed review set for Exam 1.

qualityControlNotes: Problem 1 checked: u+2v=(4,5,2), dot=-1 so angle is obtuse.
Problem 4 checked: T(2,-1)=(0,5), det=-5, area scale=5.
Problem 5 checked: det(M(k))=-4k-1, non-invertible at k=-1/4.
reviewSummary: Session 09 is the first exam review session. It has five mixed original problems covering Sections 1-4, with simple board-first explanations and exact visual specs.
